message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (for a code change the named test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if reverted; for a doc/decision item, judge whether the documented claim is now accurate/honest). Repo /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix H-016 in src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py
What: documented Firewall B is enforced STRUCTURALLY (held-out-only corpus); assert_confirm_only is the study's extra guard.
Test(s): (doc; no test)
Read the actual changed file(s); read the test; check no regression. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN. Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	"""Discovery runner — Firewall B (discover/confirm separation) enforced by construction.
2	
3	The mirror of :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.adjudication`. In discovery mode the data surfaces
4	candidate hypotheses; Firewall B (METHODOLOGY.md §2, ADR-0005) requires that the data used to
5	*discover* a hypothesis is not the data used to *confirm* it — otherwise discovering a pattern
6	and "testing" it on the same data proves nothing.
7	
8	The engine had the split primitive (:class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`) and a confirm step that
9	refuses discovery ids, but no composition — so the separation held only if a study wired it by
10	hand (same risk Firewall A had before ADR-0008). :func:`discover_and_confirm` makes it
11	structural: it hands the ``discover`` step a corpus containing *only* the discovery partition
12	and the ``confirm`` step a corpus containing *only* the held-out partition, so neither step can
13	accidentally use the other's data.
14	
15	Honest scope (lesson from the adjudication review, ADR-0008): the engine partitions the
16	**structural** data — units, and relations whose *both* endpoints are in the partition. It
17	does NOT carry the corpus's free-form ``metadata`` into a subset (an arbitrary mapping cannot
18	be partitioned safely — a per-unit index in metadata would otherwise leak the other partition
19	wholesale), and it cannot police free-text inside ``Unit``/``Relation.attributes`` that an
20	adapter might use to reference units outside the partition. So: the structural separation is
21	enforced; keeping per-unit data out of metadata/attribute *payloads* is the adapter's
22	obligation (the Firewall-B analogue of the corpus-content caveat in ADR-0005). And, as with
23	adjudication, this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping,
24	not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could).
25	"""
26	
27	from __future__ import annotations
28	
29	import datetime as _dt
30	from typing import Callable, Iterable, Protocol
31	
32	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
33	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
34	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
35	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
36	    TimestampAuthority,
37	    require_preregistered,
38	)
39	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
40	
41	
42	class HeldOutConfirmer(Protocol):
43	    """Study-supplied: confirm one locked, data-surfaced hypothesis on the held-out corpus."""
44	
45	    def __call__(self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, held_out: Corpus) -> Verdict: ...
46	
47	
48	def subset_corpus(corpus: Corpus, ids: Iterable[str]) -> Corpus:
49	    """Return the sub-corpus of ``corpus`` restricted to ``ids``.
50	
51	    Units not in ``ids`` are dropped; a relation is kept only if *both* endpoints are in
52	    ``ids`` (a relation to a unit outside the partition would leak that unit's existence/links
53	    into the partition).
54	
55	    Corpus-level ``metadata`` is deliberately **dropped**, not carried through: it is an
56	    arbitrary mapping the engine cannot partition safely. A per-unit index living in metadata
57	    (e.g. ``{"by_unit": {unit_id: ...}}``) would otherwise hand the discovery step the held-out
58	    partition wholesale — silently defeating Firewall B. Adapters that need partition-scoped
59	    side data must put it on the ``Unit``/``Relation`` records (which *are* partitioned), not in
60	    corpus metadata; free-text inside ``attributes`` that references foreign units remains the
61	    adapter's obligation (ADR-0005 / ADR-0008 honest-scope caveat).
62	    """
63	    keep = frozenset(ids)
64	    units = tuple(u for u in corpus.units if u.unit_id in keep)
65	    relations = tuple(r for r in corpus.relations if r.source_id in keep and r.target_id in keep)
66	    return Corpus(units=units, relations=relations)
67	
68	
69	def discover_and_confirm(
70	    corpus: Corpus,
71	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
72	    *,
73	    discover: Callable[[Corpus], Iterable[Hypothesis]],
74	    confirm: HeldOutConfirmer,
75	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
76	) -> list[Verdict]:
77	    """Run discovery then confirmation with the data separation enforced by construction.
78	
79	    1. ``discover`` is handed a corpus containing only the discovery partition and returns
80	       candidate hypotheses. They must be ``DISCOVERY``-origin and genuinely **pre-registered**
81	       before confirmation — ``require_preregistered`` checks (via ``authority``) that the proof
82	       binds each hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes confirmation.
83	       Discovering and confirming on the same data, or confirming a hypothesis whose content was
84	       changed after locking, is the double-dipping Firewall B exists to prevent.
85	    2. ``confirm`` is handed a corpus containing only the held-out partition and tests each
86	       hypothesis there; the verdict must report the hypothesis it answers.
87	
88	    Firewall B is enforced STRUCTURALLY here (pass 5, #H-016): the ``confirm`` step physically
89	    receives a held-out-only corpus, so it cannot evaluate a discovery-partition unit even if it
90	    wanted to — discovery ids are simply not present. The per-id ``DiscoverConfirmSplit.
91	    assert_confirm_only`` check used by :func:`confirm_unit_level` is an ADDITIONAL belt-and-braces
92	    guard a study layers inside its own confirmer; this runner does not (and cannot) call it
93	    because it does not know which evaluated ids the study's confirmer touched.
94	
95	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
96	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
97	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default. A study's ``discover`` step locks each
98	    discovered hypothesis (e.g. via :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.
99	    lock_hypothesis`) before returning it.
100	
101	    Returns the verdicts. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl. ``PreRegistrationError``) on an
102	    empty discovery or held-out partition (split ids that select no corpus units — a vacuous
103	    test), a non-``DISCOVERY`` hypothesis, a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered
104	    before confirmation, or a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution.
105	    """
106	    discovery_corpus = subset_corpus(corpus, split.discovery_ids)
107	    if not discovery_corpus.units:
108	        # the discovery partition selects no actual corpus units — a hypothesis "discovered"
109	        # from zero data is not data-surfaced at all; it would confirm cleanly on the held-out
110	        # set and silently launder a hand-authored claim through Firewall B. Fail loud.
111	        raise FirewallViolation(
112	            "discovery partition selects no corpus units — nothing to discover from "
113	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
114	        )
115	    hypotheses = list(discover(discovery_corpus))  # sees only the discovery partition
116	    if not hypotheses:
117	        # discovery surfaced nothing — a discovery run with zero hypotheses produces no
118	        # confirmatory verdict at all and is indistinguishable from a silently broken
119	        # ``discover`` callable that forgot to return. Fail loud rather than complete vacuously
120	        # (pass 3, #F-042; mirrors the empty-partition guards above).
121	        raise FirewallViolation(
122	            "discover() returned no hypotheses — a discovery run with zero hypotheses is "
123	            "vacuous and cannot produce a confirmatory verdict"
124	        )
125	
126	    held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)  # disjoint from discovery (split invariant)
127	    if not held_out.units:
128	        # the confirmation partition selects no actual corpus units — confirming on an empty
129	        # held-out set is a vacuous test (the split ids don't match the corpus). Fail loud.
130	        raise FirewallViolation(
131	            "confirmation partition selects no corpus units — nothing to confirm on "
132	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
133	        )
134	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
135	    # within-run hypothesis uniqueness (pass 3, #F-018): a ``discover`` step returning the same
136	    # hypothesis_id twice would append two verdicts for one hypothesis, inflating the verdict
137	    # count. The pipeline runner guards this via ``_require_unique_ids``; mirror it in the
138	    # public standalone runner so both entry points enforce the same identity invariant.
139	    seen_hypothesis_ids: set[str] = set()
140	    for hypothesis in hypotheses:
141	        if hypothesis.hypothesis_id in seen_hypothesis_ids:
142	            raise FirewallViolation(
143	                f"duplicate hypothesis_id {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} returned by discover() — "
144	                "each discovered hypothesis must have a unique id"
145	            )
146	        seen_hypothesis_ids.add(hypothesis.hypothesis_id)
147	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY:
148	            raise FirewallViolation(
149	                f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is {hypothesis.origin.value!r}; the "
150	                "discovery runner expects DISCOVERY-origin (data-surfaced) hypotheses"
151	            )
152	        # Pre-registration enforced by the runner: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content
153	        # (no post-lock content swap) and its attested lock time must precede this confirmation.
154	        require_preregistered(
155	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
156	        )
157	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)
158	        # A confirmer that forgets to `return` yields None; accessing verdict.hypothesis_id would
159	        # raise an opaque AttributeError instead of a typed firewall error (pass 4, #G-011 —
160	        # mirrors the pipeline/adjudication guards from #F-021).
161	        if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict):
162	            raise FirewallViolation(
163	                f"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis "
164	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — expected a Verdict"
165	            )
166	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
167	            raise FirewallViolation(
168	                f"verdict reports hypothesis_id {verdict.hypothesis_id!r} for hypothesis "
169	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
170	            )
171	        verdicts.append(verdict)
172	    return verdicts
173

content.text:
This is the H-016 commit. Let me verify the documented claim matches the actual code behavior. The key claims:

1. Firewall B is enforced STRUCTURALLY (held-out-only corpus to confirm step)
2. `assert_confirm_only` is an ADDITIONAL guard a study layers in its own confirmer
3. The runner does not (and cannot) call it because it doesn't know which evaluated ids the confirmer touched

Let me verify these claims against the actual code and the referenced `assert_confirm_only`.

content.content:
91:class DiscoverConfirmSplit:
126:    def assert_confirm_only(self, ids: Iterable[str]) -> None:

commit a9f3a487b6564f684ed056c7a57c24c674833d3d
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 01:16:12 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p5): batch A — methodology integrity / pre-registration firewall (H-001,002,003,015,016)
    
    Pass-5 confirmed findings (2-agent verified after self-refutation; revert-discriminated):
    
    - H-001 (high) close threshold-HARKing: alpha and stability_threshold are now optional,
      digest-bound Hypothesis fields. When pre-registered, the confirmers cross-check the
      confirm-time argument against the locked value (mismatch raises) — the analyst can no
      longer pick the significance level or stability bar after seeing the null/baseline. This
      is the threshold analogue of the #G-001 direction guard. Non-breaking: unlocked
      thresholds keep current caller-supplied behavior (exploratory/opted-out).
    - H-002 (med) `powered` recorded in verdict evidence + documented as a trusted caller
      obligation (the engine can't compute power from an opaque statistic, but the verdict's
      dependence on the flag is now auditable).
    - H-003 (med) documented that `test_name` is bound in the proof for audit but the engine
      receives opaque statistics and cannot verify the executed test matches it (caller obligation).
    - H-015 (low) documented that unit-level deliberately does NOT mirror whole-corpus's
      directionless-lock refusal — the locked direction never drives the unit-level verdict
      (#G-017), so a directionless lock there is harmless, not a HARKing vector.
    - H-016 (low) documented that Firewall B is enforced STRUCTURALLY in discover_and_confirm
      (confirm receives a held-out-only corpus); assert_confirm_only is the study's extra guard.
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; methodology suites green.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

 src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py            |  50 +-
 src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py          |   7 +
 src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py         |  16 +
 src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py    |   4 +

    Pass-5 confirmed findings (2-agent verified after self-refutation; revert-discriminated):
    
    - H-001 (high) close threshold-HARKing: alpha and stability_threshold are now optional,
      digest-bound Hypothesis fields. When pre-registered, the confirmers cross-check the
      confirm-time argument against the locked value (mismatch raises) — the analyst can no
      longer pick the significance level or stability bar after seeing the null/baseline. This
      is the threshold analogue of the #G-001 direction guard. Non-breaking: unlocked
      thresholds keep current caller-supplied behavior (exploratory/opted-out).
    - H-002 (med) `powered` recorded in verdict evidence + documented as a trusted caller
      obligation (the engine can't compute power from an opaque statistic, but the verdict's
      dependence on the flag is now auditable).
    - H-003 (med) documented that `test_name` is bound in the proof for audit but the engine
      receives opaque statistics and cannot verify the executed test matches it (caller obligation).
    - H-015 (low) documented that unit-level deliberately does NOT mirror whole-corpus's
      directionless-lock refusal — the locked direction never drives the unit-level verdict
      (#G-017), so a directionless lock there is harmless, not a HARKing vector.
    - H-016 (low) documented that Firewall B is enforced STRUCTURALLY in discover_and_confirm
      (confirm receives a held-out-only corpus); assert_confirm_only is the study's extra guard.
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; methodology suites green.

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py b/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py
index 863a0ab..3346b33 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py
@@ -85,6 +85,13 @@ def discover_and_confirm(
     2. ``confirm`` is handed a corpus containing only the held-out partition and tests each
        hypothesis there; the verdict must report the hypothesis it answers.
 
+    Firewall B is enforced STRUCTURALLY here (pass 5, #H-016): the ``confirm`` step physically
+    receives a held-out-only corpus, so it cannot evaluate a discovery-partition unit even if it
+    wanted to — discovery ids are simply not present. The per-id ``DiscoverConfirmSplit.
+    assert_confirm_only`` check used by :func:`confirm_unit_level` is an ADDITIONAL belt-and-braces
+    guard a study layers inside its own confirmer; this runner does not (and cannot) call it
+    because it does not know which evaluated ids the study's confirmer touched.
+
     ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
     :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
     adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default. A study's ``discover`` step locks each

85	                "Firewall A: external claims accessed during blind baseline construction"
86	            )
87	        return claims_source
88	
89	
90	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
91	class DiscoverConfirmSplit:
92	    """Firewall B. Disjoint discovery / confirmation id partitions for unit-level claims."""
93	
94	    discovery_ids: frozenset[str]
95	    confirm_ids: frozenset[str]
96	
97	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
98	        # Coerce to frozenset so the raw constructor (public API) cannot yield a "frozen" record
99	        # backed by mutable sets — otherwise the disjointness invariant below could be defeated by
100	        # post-construction in-place mutation (s.confirm_ids.add(...)) (#135).
101	        if not isinstance(self.discovery_ids, frozenset):
102	            object.__setattr__(self, "discovery_ids", frozenset(self.discovery_ids))
103	        if not isinstance(self.confirm_ids, frozenset):
104	            object.__setattr__(self, "confirm_ids", frozenset(self.confirm_ids))
105	        overlap = self.discovery_ids & self.confirm_ids
106	        if overlap:
107	            raise FirewallViolation(
108	                f"Firewall B: discovery and confirmation partitions overlap on "
109	                f"{len(overlap)} id(s), e.g. {sorted(overlap)[:3]}"
110	            )
111	        # An empty confirmation partition means there is nothing held out to confirm on; a
112	        # unit-level held-out split with no held-out object is invalid by construction. The
113	        # genuine no-holdout case must use the whole-corpus null path (audit pass 1, issue #5).
114	        if not self.confirm_ids:
115	            raise FirewallViolation(
116	                "Firewall B: confirmation partition is empty — a unit-level held-out split "
117	                "must hold out at least one object (use whole-corpus confirmation otherwise)"
118	            )
119	
120	    @classmethod
121	    def from_partition(
122	        cls, discovery_ids: Iterable[str], confirm_ids: Iterable[str]
123	    ) -> "DiscoverConfirmSplit":
124	        return cls(frozenset(discovery_ids), frozenset(confirm_ids))
125	
126	    def assert_confirm_only(self, ids: Iterable[str]) -> None:
127	        """Raise if ``ids`` is empty or any of it was seen during discovery (double-dip)."""
128	        ids = frozenset(ids)
129	        if not ids:
130	            raise FirewallViolation(
131	                "Firewall B: confirmatory test evaluated zero held-out objects — a verdict "
132	                "cannot be produced from an empty confirmation set (audit pass 1, issue #5)"
133	            )
134	        leaked = ids & self.discovery_ids
135	        if leaked:
136	            raise FirewallViolation(
137	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test touched {len(leaked)} discovery id(s) — "
138	                f"double-dipping, e.g. {sorted(leaked)[:3]}"
139	            )
140	        unknown = ids - self.confirm_ids
141	        if unknown:
142	            raise FirewallViolation(
143	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test used {len(unknown)} id(s) outside the "
144	                f"confirmation partition, e.g. {sorted(unknown)[:3]}"
145	            )
146

content.text: Now let me verify the structural claim concretely: that `subset_corpus` is actually called with `confirm_ids` and the resulting held-out corpus is what's passed to `confirm`. And verify `confirm_unit_level` actually uses `assert_confirm_only`.

content.content:
src/assay_engine/methodology/firewalls.py:126:    def assert_confirm_only(self, ids: Iterable[str]) -> None:
src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:206:    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)

content.text: Let me look at confirm_unit_level around line 206 to confirm assert_confirm_only is used by it (the doc says ":func:`confirm_unit_level`").

content.content:
175	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
176	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
177	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
178	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
179	
180	    Consequently (pass 5, #H-015) the unit-level path deliberately does NOT mirror
181	    :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`'s directionless-lock REFUSAL: because the locked direction never
182	    drives the unit-level verdict, a directionless lock here is harmless, not a HARKing vector —
183	    the asymmetry is intentional, not a missing guard.
184	
185	    Test identity (pass 5, #H-003): ``test_name`` is bound into the pre-registration proof for
186	    audit, but the engine receives only an opaque ``statistic``/``p_value`` and CANNOT verify that
187	    the test actually executed matches the locked ``test_name`` — that remains a caller obligation.
188	    """
189	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
190	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
191	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
192	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
193	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
194	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
195	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
196	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
197	    # against an unrecognized tail.
198	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
199	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
200	    ):
201	        raise ValueError(
202	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
203	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
204	        )
205	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
206	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
207	    alpha = cast(float, _resolve_locked_param(hypothesis.alpha, alpha, "alpha"))  # #H-001
208	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
209	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,

content.text: Confirmed. The function at line 206 is `confirm_unit_level` and it calls `split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)`. Let me confirm the function name at the def.

All claims in the H-016 doc are verified accurate:

1. **"Firewall B is enforced STRUCTURALLY ... confirm step physically receives a held-out-only corpus"** — TRUE. Lines 126, 157: `held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)` then `confirm(hypothesis, held_out)`. `subset_corpus` drops all units not in `confirm_ids` (line 64), so discovery-partition units are not present. The split's `__post_init__` enforces disjointness (firewalls.py:105-110), so confirm_ids cannot overlap discovery_ids.

2. **"discovery ids are simply not present"** — TRUE by subset_corpus construction.

3. **"`DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` check used by `confirm_unit_level`"** — TRUE. confirm.py:206 inside `confirm_unit_level` (def at line 155).

4. **"is an ADDITIONAL belt-and-braces guard a study layers inside its own confirmer; this runner does not (and cannot) call it because it does not know which evaluated ids the study's confirmer touched"** — TRUE. The runner takes an opaque `HeldOutConfirmer` (Protocol, line 42-45) returning only a `Verdict`; it never receives the set of evaluated ids, so it genuinely cannot call `assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)`. `assert_confirm_only` requires the caller to pass the ids actually evaluated — info the runner doesn't have.

This is a doc-only change (+7 lines, no test, as stated). The honest-scope caveats elsewhere in the docstring (metadata dropping, signature-level guarantee) are consistent. The claim is accurate and honest — it correctly distinguishes the structural guarantee (always active in this runner) from the per-id guard (study's responsibility), and is candid about why the runner cannot apply the latter. No regression possible from a docstring addition; gates reported clean.

input.reasoning:
Doc-only change (+7 lines, lines 88-93 of src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py); commit a9f3a48. As a doc/decision item the bar is whether the documented claim is now accurate/honest — it is, on every point, verified against code:

1. STRUCTURAL enforcement: discover_and_confirm computes held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids) (line 126) and passes it to confirm() (line 157). subset_corpus (lines 48-66) keeps only units whose unit_id is in confirm_ids and drops the rest, so discovery-partition units are physically absent from the confirm corpus. DiscoverConfirmSplit.__post_init__ (firewalls.py:105-110) enforces disjointness of discovery_ids/confirm_ids, so the held-out-only claim holds. "discovery ids are simply not present" is correct.

2. The reference to DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only "used by confirm_unit_level" is accurate: assert_confirm_only is defined firewalls.py:126 and called at confirm.py:206, inside confirm_unit_level (def at confirm.py:155). Grep shows that is the only production caller.

3. "this runner does not (and cannot) call it because it does not know which evaluated ids the confirmer touched" is honest: HeldOutConfirmer is an opaque Protocol (lines 42-45) returning only a Verdict; the runner never receives the set of evaluated ids that assert_confirm_only(ids) requires, so it genuinely cannot invoke it. Calling it the study's ADDITIONAL belt-and-braces guard layered inside its own confirmer is correct.

The new paragraph is consistent with the surrounding honest-scope caveats (metadata dropping, signature-level not process-isolation guarantee). No test is required for a docstring, and a docstring addition cannot regress behavior; commit message reports ruff/mypy/methodology suites green. Both appropriate and complete.

content.text: CONFIRMED. The H-016 docstring addition in discovery.py accurately and honestly documents that Firewall B is enforced structurally (confirm step receives a held-out-only corpus via subset_corpus on confirm_ids, with split disjointness enforced), and correctly frames assert_confirm_only as the study's additional per-id guard that the opaque runner cannot itself call. All claims verified against the code; no regression possible from a doc-only change.
